title: Geometry from a Time Series
abstract: Shows that the geometry of a strange attractor can be reconstructed from a single time series, laying the experimental groundwork for reconstructing nonlinear dynamics from data — the empirical precursor to inferring structure and computation from observed processes.

title: Inferring Statistical Complexity
abstract: Introduces the ε-machine — the minimal causal-state representation of a stationary stochastic process — and the statistical complexity, a measure of the memory required to produce a process. The founding paper of computational mechanics.

title: Computation at the Onset of Chaos
venue: Entropy, Complexity and Physics of Information
abstract: Applies ε-machine reconstruction to the logistic map near the period-doubling accumulation point, showing that statistical complexity diverges at the onset of chaos — connecting computation-theoretic structure to dynamical phase transitions.

title: The Attractor-Basin Portrait of a Cellular Automaton
abstract: Extends ε-machine reconstruction to one-dimensional cellular automata, identifying domains, particles, and particle interactions as the emergent computational primitives of spatially extended dynamical systems.

title: Evolving Cellular Automata with Genetic Algorithms
abstract: Uses genetic algorithms to evolve cellular automata rules that perform global computational tasks, studying how structured emergent computation arises under selection. A bridge between evolutionary computation and spatial computational mechanics.

title: Measures of Statistical Complexity: Why?
abstract: Critically compares candidate measures of statistical complexity (e.g., statistical complexity C_μ, effective measure complexity) and shows which satisfy basic desiderata — sharpening what 'the structure of a process' means quantitatively.

title: Computational Mechanics: Pattern and Prediction, Structure and Simplicity
abstract: The canonical modern treatment: a rigorous construction of the ε-machine via causal equivalence of histories, with proofs that the causal-state representation is the unique minimal optimal predictor. Anchors computational mechanics as a formal program.

title: Discovering Planar Disorder in Close-Packed Structures from X-Ray Diffraction
abstract: Reconstructs ε-machines from X-ray diffraction data of layered close-packed materials, yielding the first direct experimental application of computational mechanics to materials structure — turning diffraction into an inference problem about stochastic stacking processes.

title: Blind Construction of Optimal Nonlinear Recursive Predictors for Discrete Sequences
abstract: Introduces CSSR (Causal-State Splitting Reconstruction), a consistent algorithm for inferring ε-machines from finite samples of discrete symbol sequences. The first practical, scalable inference method for computational mechanics.

title: Automatic Filters for the Detection of Coherent Structure in Spatiotemporal Systems
abstract: Generalizes causal-state reconstruction to space-time fields, automatically detecting coherent structures (domains, particles) from raw simulation data without hand-designed filters. Operationalizes spatial computational mechanics.

title: Time's Barbed Arrow: Irreversibility, Crypticity, and Stored Information
abstract: Shows that a process and its time-reversal generically have different ε-machines, introducing crypticity — the information stored in the present that is hidden from the future — and revealing a new structural asymmetry of stochastic dynamics.

title: Prediction, Retrodiction, and the Amount of Information Stored in the Present
abstract: Decomposes the information stored by a process into ephemeral, bound, and enigmatic components, giving a unified information-theoretic accounting of how a process remembers, predicts, and hides.

title: Equivalence of History and Generator ε-Machines
abstract: Proves the equivalence between the history-based and generator-based definitions of the ε-machine — closing a long-standing gap in the foundations and clarifying that the two constructive routes to causal states produce the same object.

title: Bayesian Structural Inference for Hidden Processes
abstract: A fully Bayesian method for inferring ε-machines from data that quantifies uncertainty over model structure itself, not just parameters. Addresses model selection in computational mechanics via explicit posteriors over process topology.

title: Occam's Quantum Strop: Synchronizing and Compressing Classical Cryptic Processes via a Quantum Channel
abstract: Constructs quantum implementations of classical ε-machines that achieve strictly lower memory costs than the classical optimum, demonstrating that quantum representations can break the classical statistical-complexity bound.

title: Identifying Functional Thermodynamics in Autonomous Maxwellian Ratchets
abstract: Uses the ε-machine of the input tape to classify autonomous Maxwell-demon ratchets into erasers, engines, and dissipators, revealing how structural complexity of a memory tape determines what thermodynamic function a demon can perform.

title: Nearly Maximally Predictive Features and Their Dimensions
abstract: Characterizes the tradeoff between predictive accuracy and feature dimension for continuous-time processes, extending rate-distortion ideas into computational mechanics and bounding how much structure can be compressed while preserving prediction.

title: Thermodynamics of Modularity: Structural Costs Beyond the Landauer Bound
abstract: Shows that decomposing a computation into modules incurs dissipation strictly above Landauer's bound, with the excess expressible in terms of the ε-machines of the sub-processes. Identifies 'modularity dissipation' as a fundamental thermodynamic cost of structured computation.

title: Spectral Simplicity of Apparent Complexity: The Complete Analysis of Spectral Decompositions
abstract: Gives closed-form expressions for the information-theoretic functionals of hidden Markov processes via spectral decomposition of the mixed-state presentation, making exact analytic computation of ε-machine information measures tractable for the first time.

title: Strong and Weak Optimizations in Classical and Quantum Models of Stochastic Processes
abstract: Characterizes when quantum models can outperform the classical ε-machine in memory, distinguishing strong versus weak optimization regimes and identifying structural features of a process that make quantum advantage possible.
